pdf_info.Title: Secure Parsing and Serializing with Separation Logic Applied to CBOR, CDDL, and COSE
pdf_info.Subject: -  Software and its engineering  ->  Source code generation.Specification languages.Correctness.Formal software verification.-  Information systems  ->  Data layout.Data encoding and canonicalization.-  Security and privacy  ->  Key management.Embedded systems security.Trusted computing.Management and querying of encrypted data.
pdf_info.Keywords: Binary data formats, CBOR, CDDL, COSE, DICE, DPE, Formal verification, Measured boot, Secrets management
pdf_info.Author: Tahina Ramananandro; Gabriel Ebner; Guido Martínez; Nikhil Swamy
pdf_info.Creator: arXiv GenPDF (tex2pdf:)

contributions.statement: PulseParse, a new verified library for secure parsing and serialization
EverCBOR, a formalization of CBOR, including a proof that its deterministic encoding is indeed non-malleable—the first such proof
a formalization of CDDL, including inferring well-formedness conditions on CDDL definitions (in the form of a new elaboration algorithm, proven sound) that yield unambiguous and non-malleable formats
to evaluate our libraries on two applications, COSE and DPE

probes: verified library for secure parsing and serialization
matched_probes: verified library for secure parsing and serialization
claims.excerpt: Towards this end, we present PulseParse, a library of verified parser and serializer combinators for non-malleable binary formats.
PulseParse also supports a class of recursive formats—with a focus on security and handling adversarial inputs, we show how to parse such formats with only a constant amount of stack space.
Towards this end, we present PulseParse, a library of verified parser and serializer combinators for non-malleable binary formats.

We identify well-formedness conditions on CDDL definitions that ensure that they yield unambiguous, non-malleable formats, and implement EverCDDL, a tool that checks that a CDDL definition is well-formed, and then produces verified parsers and serializers for it.

Secure Parsing and Serializing with Separation Logic Applied to CBOR, CDDL, and COSE TAHINA RAMANANANDRO, GABRIEL EBNER, GUIDO MARTÍNEZ, and NIKHIL SWAMY, Microsoft arXiv:2505.17335v2 [cs.CR] 17 Jul 2025 Research, USA Incorrect handling of security-critical data formats, particularly in low-level languages, are the root cause of many security vulnerabilities.
We also use our toolchain to generate verified code for other standards specified in CDDL, including DICE Protection Environment, a secure boot protocol standard.
2025], with formal proofs of memory safety, functional correctness, and non-malleability (i.e., unique binary representation) of formats.

Towards this end, we present PulseParse, a library of verified parser and serializer combinators for non-malleable binary formats.

We identify well-formedness conditions on CDDL definitions that ensure that they yield unambiguous, non-malleable formats, and implement EverCDDL, a tool that checks that a CDDL definition is well-formed, and then produces verified parsers and serializers for it.
combinators + Recursion §3: EverCBOR Verified CBOR Library CBOR.c CBOR.rs §4: EverCDDL Verified Code Generator for CDDL Karamel code generator from F* to C/Rust (Protzenko et al.

We evaluate EverCBOR against commonly used unverified CBOR libraries such as QCBOR [Lundblade 2023] and TinyCBOR [Intel 2021], noting that we support more CBOR features (including arbitrary maps), and implement all necessary checks, find that our verified code is competitive in speed and memory consumption.
